text:
Single atom catalysts (SACs) with well-defined single metal sites are of great interest to reveal the underlying catalytic mechanism and the active sites of catalysts. However, elucidating the *operando* electronic and structural state of single metal sites under catalytic conditions is a great challenge due to the fact that almost all the site-specific *operando* techniques are hard to be integrated into well-studied ideal SAC model catalysts. Here, for the first time, a combination of multiple advanced *operando* techniques is employed to elucidate the structural evolution of the active sites in the high-spin (HS) Fe(III)N₄ sites of a model catalyst during the CO₂ reduction reaction in the electrochemical condition.

Combining operando57Fe Mössbauer and X-ray absorption spectroscopies, we observe a potential-dependent change in the species present on the electrode from the high-spin (HS) Fe(III)N4 structure to a HS Fe(II)N4 structure, and a significant influence of the electrolyte electrode environment arising from a change in species adsorbed to the electrode surface upon switching from CO2-saturated solution to Ar-saturated solution. Operando Raman spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry indicates that changes in the phthalocyanine (Pc) ligand core also occur, ranging from iron (II) phthalocyanine, Fe(II)Pc, to the reduced Fe(II)Pc− species. Identification of the catalytic species responsible for the acceleration of CO2RR is the iron in the form of the high-spin (HS) Fe(II)Pc− species. Furthermore, density functional theory studies demonstrate that the electroreduction of the ligand occurs over the potential window where the catalytic current is observed, and which causes a rearrangement in the geometry of the molecule, resulting in a change in the d-band centre of the formed in situ FeII species that favours CO2 binding and significantly enhances catalytic current. This research provides the first multi-technique experimental and theoretical analysis of the electronic structure of the catalytic centre of the SAC.
